unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you will learn about the world around us — the plants, the trees, the air, and the land and water we share. You will find out how to care for planet Earth by planting, watering, picking up litter, and recycling. You will learn the parts of a plant, why plants and trees are so important, and how to say thank you for the beautiful things in nature. By the end, you will be ready to make your own promise to care for the Earth, just like Samira does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher audiovisual lecture and learn a few vocabulary words each day.
Complete all six grammar practices, moving from guided examples to independent use.
Listen to the Unit readings, including Samira and the Little Tree, and answer the comprehension questions.
Complete all six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question quiz and student self-assessment.

visual.alt: Children planting, recycling and caring for nature

readings.title: Mother Earth Day
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Mother Earth Day is a special day when people all around the world show they care about our planet. On this day, people do many good things. Some people plant trees and flowers, because trees and plants help clean the air we breathe. Some people pick up litter from parks, beaches and streets, to keep the Earth clean and safe for animals. Other people learn about recycling, which helps save trees and water. Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet!

readings.title: Plants and Clean Air
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Plants are very important for our planet. The green leaves of trees and plants clean the dirty air. They take in a gas called carbon dioxide and give out oxygen. Oxygen is what we need to breathe. This is why big forests are called the “lungs of the Earth” — they help the Earth breathe! Plants can clean the air inside our homes and schools too, and they make our spaces more beautiful. Taking care of plants is a way of taking care of ourselves.

readings.title: A Family on Mother Earth Day
readings.passageScript:
Listen to this story about one family. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“Today is Mother Earth Day. Ishmael and his family are doing different things to help the environment. Ishmael’s father is planting trees with his uncle. His mother is counting birds with his aunt. Ishmael’s brother is picking up litter with his friends. Ishmael is making paper flowers with his grandma from old newspapers. The family promise to do something good for the Earth every day, not only on Mother Earth Day.”

readings.title: A Poem for the Earth
readings.passageScript:
Listen, then read along:
I’m glad the sky is painted blue,
And Earth is painted green,
With such a lot of nice fresh air
All sandwiched in between.

readings.title: Samira and the Little Tree
readings.genre: A gentle nature story with dialogue.
readings.theme: Caring for the Earth — planting, watering, and keeping our places clean.
readings.setting: A small village near a dry river in East Africa.
readings.passageScript:
Samira lived in a small village near a river. She loved to sit under the old tree by her house. Its green leaves gave her cool shade, and birds sang in its branches every morning.
One hot day, Samira saw litter all along the path to the river. Old bags and papers lay in the soil.
“Look at all this litter,” said Samira to her brother Ishmael. “Our village is not clean.”
“You are right,” said Ishmael. “Let us pick it up.”
Samira and Ishmael spent the morning picking up the litter. They put the paper in a box for recycling. Their friend Selma came to help too.
“When we recycle this paper,” said Selma, “fewer trees are cut down. Recycling helps the Earth.”
Soon the path was clean. Samira felt happy. “I’m glad that our village looks beautiful again,” she said.
But Samira looked sad when she saw the river. It was almost dry. Along the bank, where tall trees once grew, the land was empty and brown.
“Where did all the trees go?” asked Samira.
Her grandmother, Amal, was sitting nearby. “People cut them down for wood,” she said softly. “Without the trees, the sun dried the earth, and the river grew small. Trees clean the air and hold the water in the soil.”
“Then we must plant new trees!” said Samira.
Grandmother Amal smiled. She gave Samira a handful of small seeds. “Here,” she said. “Little things can make a big difference.”
The next morning, Samira and Ishmael dug small holes in the soft soil. Adam, the boy from next door, ran over to watch.
“What are you doing?” asked Adam.
“We are planting seeds,” said Samira. “These seeds will grow into tall trees one day.”
“Can I help?” asked Adam.
“Yes! You can be my partner,” said Samira, laughing.
Every morning, Samira was watering the little seeds. She watched the soil carefully. One day, a tiny green stem pushed up from the ground. Then came two small leaves.
“Look, Ishmael!” cried Samira. “The roots are growing under the ground, and the stem is growing up to the sky!”
The children looked after their plants all through the dry season. Adam carried water. Selma pulled out the weeds. Slowly, the little trees grew taller and stronger.
After many months, one small tree grew its very first flower. It was white and soft, and it smelled sweet.
“Our tree has a flower!” said Samira. “Soon there will be seeds, and we can plant even more trees.”
The whole village came to look. The children had planted a row of little trees along the river bank. Birds began to visit again. The air smelled fresh and clean.
Grandmother Amal put her hand on Samira’s shoulder. “You cared for the Earth,” she said, “and the Earth is saying thank you.”
That night, Samira looked at the young trees under the moon. “I’m thankful for the trees, the water and the clean air,” she whispered. “I promise to take care of you always.”
And every day after that, Samira remembered her grandmother’s words: little things can make a big difference.

comprehension.explanation: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

grammar.title: Present Continuous (actions happening now)
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about something that is happening right now. In this unit we use it to say what people are doing to help the Earth — for example, planting, watering or picking up litter.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use am / is / are together with the verb + -ing.
Use am with I:  “I am planting a tree.”
Use is with he, she, it:  “She is watering the flowers.”
Use are with you, we, they:  “They are picking up litter.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She watering the plants.”  ✗
Fix: We must not forget the little helping word: “She is watering the plants.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of two parts every time: a helper (am/is/are) + a verb ending in -ing. One without the other is not finished!
grammar.practice:
Choose am, is or are.
I ____ (am / is / are) planting seeds.
He ____ (am / is / are) watering the garden.
The children ____ (am / is / are) picking up litter.
We ____ (am / is / are) recycling old paper.
She ____ (am / is / are) counting the birds.

grammar.title: Present Continuous (actions happening now): Use It
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about something that is happening right now. In this unit we use it to say what people are doing to help the Earth — for example, planting, watering or picking up litter. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 7 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use am / is / are together with the verb + -ing.
Use am with I:  “I am planting a tree.”
Use is with he, she, it:  “She is watering the flowers.”
Use are with you, we, they:  “They are picking up litter.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She watering the plants.”  ✗
Fix: We must not forget the little helping word: “She is watering the plants.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of two parts every time: a helper (am/is/are) + a verb ending in -ing. One without the other is not finished!
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Saying Thank You for Nature
grammar.explanation: We can use special phrases to show that we are happy about, or thankful for, the beautiful things in nature. This helps us share our feelings and be grateful.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start your sentence with one of these phrases, then say the thing you are thankful for:
“I’m glad that…”  →  “I’m glad that the sky is blue.”
“I’m happy that…”  →  “I’m happy that trees give us fresh air.”
“I appreciate…”  →  “I appreciate the beautiful flowers.”
“I’m thankful for…”  →  “I’m thankful for clean water.”
“It’s important to…”  →  “It’s important to take care of our planet.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I appreciate for the flowers.”  ✗
Fix: After appreciate we do not need “for”: “I appreciate the flowers.”  ✓  (But we do say “I’m thankful for the flowers.”)
grammar.memoryTip: “appreciate” likes to hug the thing straight away (no “for”). “thankful” always brings a “for” with it.
grammar.practice:
Finish each sentence with your own idea about nature.
I’m glad that ______.
I appreciate ______.
I’m thankful for ______.
It’s important to ______.

grammar.title: Saying Thank You for Nature: Use It
grammar.explanation: We can use special phrases to show that we are happy about, or thankful for, the beautiful things in nature. This helps us share our feelings and be grateful. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 7 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start your sentence with one of these phrases, then say the thing you are thankful for:
“I’m glad that…”  →  “I’m glad that the sky is blue.”
“I’m happy that…”  →  “I’m happy that trees give us fresh air.”
“I appreciate…”  →  “I appreciate the beautiful flowers.”
“I’m thankful for…”  →  “I’m thankful for clean water.”
“It’s important to…”  →  “It’s important to take care of our planet.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I appreciate for the flowers.”  ✗
Fix: After appreciate we do not need “for”: “I appreciate the flowers.”  ✓  (But we do say “I’m thankful for the flowers.”)
grammar.memoryTip: “appreciate” likes to hug the thing straight away (no “for”). “thankful” always brings a “for” with it.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: this / these and that / those
grammar.explanation: We use these words to point at things. In this unit we use them to point at plants, flowers and trees when we are near them or a little far away.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
this → one thing that is near:  “This plant is a tomato plant.”
these → two or more things that are near:  “These plants are onions.”
that → one thing that is far:  “That tree is very tall.”
those → two or more things that are far:  “Those flowers are red.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “This plants are onions.”  ✗
Fix: For more than one thing that is near, use these: “These plants are onions.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Near = this / these (both have a short, close sound). Far = that / those. Add the “-se/-ose” when there is more than one.
grammar.practice:
Choose the right word.
____ (This / These) flower near my hand is yellow.
____ (That / Those) trees far away are very tall.
____ (This / These) seeds in my hand are very small.
____ (That / Those) bird up in the sky is singing.

grammar.title: this / these and that / those: Use It
grammar.explanation: We use these words to point at things. In this unit we use them to point at plants, flowers and trees when we are near them or a little far away. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 7 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
this → one thing that is near:  “This plant is a tomato plant.”
these → two or more things that are near:  “These plants are onions.”
that → one thing that is far:  “That tree is very tall.”
those → two or more things that are far:  “Those flowers are red.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “This plants are onions.”  ✗
Fix: For more than one thing that is near, use these: “These plants are onions.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Near = this / these (both have a short, close sound). Far = that / those. Add the “-se/-ose” when there is more than one.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Talk About Environmental Actions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at these actions: planting trees, watering plants, picking up litter, recycling. For each one, say:
“This person is ______.” (Use the present continuous — see Grammar Lesson 1.)
“______ helps the environment because ______.”
“I can ______ to help the Earth.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use is/are + -ing correctly? Did you speak clearly?

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Name the Parts of a Plant
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at a plant or a picture of a plant. Point to each part and say:
“These are the ______.”
“The ______ help the plant to ______.”
“Without ______, the plant cannot ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Say Thank You to Nature
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think about trees, flowers, clean air, water, animals and the sky. For each one, say a sentence:
“I’m glad that ______.”
“I appreciate ______ because ______.”
“It’s important to protect ______ because ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Plant Guide
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Name the roots, stem, leaves and flower, and explain one job for each part.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Point clearly and use simple present facts.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Earth-Care Reporter
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Describe six people caring for the environment using is or are plus -ing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Include planting, watering, picking up and recycling.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - Thank-You Circle
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Say six things you appreciate in nature and explain why one matters to you.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use I'm glad that or I appreciate.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 1 — Environmental Actions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write five sentences about what people are doing to help the Earth. Then add why the action is good:
The boy is planting a tree. Trees give us clean air to breathe.
The girl is watering the flowers. Plants need water to grow.
Now write three more of your own.
writing.modelText: We care for the Earth by planting trees and picking up litter. Plants give us clean air. I am thankful for nature.
writing.sentenceStarter: We care for the Earth by planting trees and picking up litter.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Thank-You Letter to the Earth
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short letter to the Earth. Use this plan: a greeting, thank you for some special things, why they matter, a promise to help, and a closing. For example:
Dear Earth, thank you for the tall trees that give us clean air. I appreciate the clean water that helps every living thing. I promise to pick up litter and plant a tree. Your friend, ______.
writing.modelText:
Dear Earth,
Thank you for clean air, green plants and water. I will help by planting trees and picking up litter.
From, Samira
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Story response: Samira and the Little Tree
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about how you can care for the world around you. Start with: “I can ______.”
Draw Samira’s little tree with its roots, stem, leaves and flower. Label each part.
Write one sentence that says thank you to nature. Start with: “I’m thankful for ______.”
writing.modelText: Samira and Ishmael picked up litter and planted seeds. Their friends joined them. They cared for the village and helped a little tree begin to grow.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 4 - How a Plant Helps
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five facts about how plants help people, animals, soil or air.
writing.modelText: Plants help clean the air. Their roots hold soil, and their leaves provide shade.
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 5 - My Earth-Care Plan
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six actions you can take this week to care for your environment.
writing.modelText: I will carry a reusable bottle. I will put litter in a bin and help water a young tree.
writing.sentenceStarter: I will carry a reusable bottle
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.

writing.title: Writing 6 - A Thank-You Letter to Earth
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a greeting, four appreciative sentences, one promise and a closing.
writing.modelText: Dear Earth, I appreciate your clean air, trees and water. I promise to care for the places around me.
writing.sentenceStarter: Dear Earth, I appreciate your clean air, trees and water
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the Blanks (Caring for the Earth)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with an action word: planting, watering, picking up, recycling, using.
______ trees helps clean the air we breathe.
______ plants helps them grow strong and healthy.
______ litter keeps our environment clean and safe for animals.
______ paper, plastic and glass helps save resources.
______ less water helps us have enough for the future.
activities.answerSummary: 1. Planting  2. Watering  3. Picking up  4. Recycling  5. Using

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match the Plant Part to Its Job
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each plant part to what it does.
Roots  —  (a) hold up the plant and carry water up to the leaves.
Stem  —  (b) take in water from the soil.
Leaves  —  (c) make seeds for new plants.
Flower  —  (d) make food for the plant using sunlight.

activities.title: Activity 3 — True or False (Nature Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
Planting trees helps clean the air.
Roots grow above the ground.
Plants take in carbon dioxide and give out oxygen.
Plants do not need water to grow.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True  2. False (roots grow below the ground)  3. True  4. False (plants need water, or they will die)

activities.title: Activity 4 - Plant-Part Match
activities.instructionsAndItems: Match roots, stem, leaves and flower to their jobs, then label a plant.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.

quizzes.question: What does 'planting' mean?
quizzes.options: Putting seeds or young plants in the soil so they can grow. | Giving water to plants to help them grow. | Collecting things from the ground. | Rubbish that people leave on the ground in public places.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Putting seeds or young plants in the soil so they can grow.
quizzes.explanation: Planting trees helps clean the air and gives us shade.

quizzes.question: Which word means: Giving water to plants to help them grow.
quizzes.explanation: Selma is watering the flowers every morning.

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'picking up'?
quizzes.explanation: Picking up litter keeps our street clean.

quizzes.question: Please do not drop ____; use a bin instead.
quizzes.explanation: Please do not drop litter; use a bin instead.

quizzes.question: Which word means: The parts of a plant that grow underground and take in water.
quizzes.explanation: roots: The parts of a plant that grow underground and take in water. The roots of the big tree go deep into the ground.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which word means: The main part of a plant that holds up the leaves and flower.
quizzes.explanation: stem: The main part of a plant that holds up the leaves and flower. Water travels up the stem to reach the leaves.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'leaves'?
quizzes.explanation: leaves: The flat, usually green parts that grow from the stem. The green leaves make food for the plant using sunlight.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: The garden is full of beautiful ____s.
quizzes.explanation: flower: The colourful part of a plant that makes seeds. The garden is full of beautiful flowers.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: seeds: The tiny parts of a plant that can grow into new plants. We put the seeds in the soil and watered them.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

teacherNotes.note:
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the Earth poem with me and explain it?” “Can you play a listening game about what the family is doing?” “How is Mother Earth Day celebrated in East Africa?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me practise talking about environmental actions?” “Am I using the present continuous tense correctly?” “Can you help me make my thank-you sentences sound natural?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check if my sentences are correct?” “Can you help me make my thank-you letter to the Earth better?”
Story: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you check my sentences about caring for the Earth?” “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”
Grammar: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my grammar answers?” “When do I use ‘am’, ‘is’ and ‘are’?” “Can you give me five more sentences to practise this, these, that and those?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What are some ways children can help the environment?” “Why is picking up litter important?” “How is recycling done in East Africa?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What does each part of a plant do?” “Why are plants important for clean air?” “What plants are common in East Africa?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me learn more nature words?” “Why is clean water so important?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “How can I express appreciation for nature?” “Can you check if my thank-you sentences sound natural?”

Term placement approved. Outcome mappings, comprehension guidance, vocabulary enrichment, assessments, live sessions, rubrics, writing specifications and answer-key coverage completed in the approved reference model.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. Planting  2. Watering  3. Picking up  4. Recycling  5. Using
1. True  2. False (roots grow below the ground)  3. True  4. False (plants need water, or they will die)
1. am   2. is   3. are   4. are   5. is

Answers will be your own ideas. Check that each sentence keeps its phrase correct, for example: “I’m glad that the birds are singing.”  ·  “I appreciate the tall trees.”  ·  “I’m thankful for clean water.”  ·  “It’s important to plant more trees.”
1. This   2. Those   3. These   4. That

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet!
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet!

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet!
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet!

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Some people plant trees and flowers, because trees and plants help clean the air we breathe.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Some people plant trees and flowers, because trees and plants help clean the air we breathe.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Some people pick up litter from parks, beaches and streets, to keep the Earth clean and safe for animals.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Some people pick up litter from parks, beaches and streets, to keep the Earth clean and safe for animals.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet! On this day, people do many good things.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Every day can be Earth Day if we remember to care for our planet! On this day, people do many good things.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Plants can clean the air inside our homes and schools too, and they make our spaces more beautiful.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Plants can clean the air inside our homes and schools too, and they make our spaces more beautiful.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: They take in a gas called carbon dioxide and give out oxygen.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: They take in a gas called carbon dioxide and give out oxygen.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: This is why big forests are called the “lungs of the Earth” — they help the Earth breathe!
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: This is why big forests are called the “lungs of the Earth” — they help the Earth breathe!

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Taking care of plants is a way of taking care of ourselves.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Taking care of plants is a way of taking care of ourselves.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Ishmael and his family are doing different things to help the environment. Ishmael’s father is planting trees with his uncle.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Ishmael and his family are doing different things to help the environment. Ishmael’s father is planting trees with his uncle.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Ishmael and his family are doing different things to help the environment. Ishmael is making paper flowers with his grandma from old newspapers.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Ishmael and his family are doing different things to help the environment. Ishmael is making paper flowers with his grandma from old newspapers.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The family promise to do something good for the Earth every day, not only on Mother Earth Day.”
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The family promise to do something good for the Earth every day, not only on Mother Earth Day.”

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: I’m glad the sky is painted blue, And Earth is painted green, With such a lot of nice fresh air All sandwiched in between.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: I’m glad the sky is painted blue, And Earth is painted green, With such a lot of nice fresh air All sandwiched in between.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: I’m glad the sky is painted blue, And Earth is painted green, With such a lot of nice fresh air All sandwiched in between.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: I’m glad the sky is painted blue, And Earth is painted green, With such a lot of nice fresh air All sandwiched in between.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: One hot day, Samira saw litter all along the path to the river.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: One hot day, Samira saw litter all along the path to the river.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: They put the paper in a box for recycling. “When we recycle this paper,” said Selma, “fewer trees are cut down.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: They put the paper in a box for recycling. “When we recycle this paper,” said Selma, “fewer trees are cut down.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “When we recycle this paper,” said Selma, “fewer trees are cut down. It was almost dry.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “When we recycle this paper,” said Selma, “fewer trees are cut down. It was almost dry.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Grandmother Amal put her hand on Samira’s shoulder.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Grandmother Amal put her hand on Samira’s shoulder.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: One hot day, Samira saw litter all along the path to the river.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: One hot day, Samira saw litter all along the path to the river.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The children had planted a row of little trees along the river bank.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The children had planted a row of little trees along the river bank.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “I promise to take care of you always.” And every day after that, Samira remembered her grandmother’s words: little things can make a big difference. “Little things can make a big difference.” The next morning, Samira and Ishmael dug small holes in the soft soil.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “I promise to take care of you always.” And every day after that, Samira remembered her grandmother’s words: little things can make a big difference. “Little things can make a big difference.” The next morning, Samira and Ishmael dug small holes in the soft soil.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: After many months, one small tree grew its very first flower.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: After many months, one small tree grew its very first flower.

Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
Marking guidance: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.
Correct answer: Putting seeds or young plants in the soil so they can grow.. Planting trees helps clean the air and gives us shade.
Correct answer: watering. Selma is watering the flowers every morning.
Correct answer: Verb. Picking up litter keeps our street clean.
Correct answer: litter. Please do not drop litter; use a bin instead.
Correct answer: recycling. Recycling paper helps save trees.
Assess the writing with rub-writing-v1 and the recording with rub-speaking-v1. Award up to 16 marks for each artifact.
